html:
OPORF / hVIVO PLC

hVIVO is no longer just the small London human-challenge trial shop the market punished in 2025. It is becoming a specialized early-stage clinical development platform with a scarce core asset: the world-leading human challenge trial operation, its FluCamp recruitment engine, in-house virology and immunology labs, and now German early-phase clinical sites and biostorage through CRS and Cryostore.

The setup is asymmetric because the stock still trades like the 2025 disappointment is permanent. FY25 revenue fell to £46.8m from £62.7m, adjusted EBITDA collapsed to £1.4m from £16.4m, cash dropped to £14.3m, and the order book was only £30m at year-end. That was real pain, driven by infectious-disease funding weakness and canceled HCT studies. But 2025 also reset the business. hVIVO bought CRS and Cryostore, created four service lines, and shifted from pure HCT cyclicality toward an integrated CRO model spanning consulting, clinical trials, challenge trials, and laboratories. Management expects high single-digit revenue growth in 2026, weighted to H2, and says CRS should be profitable and cash generative this year.

The market is missing the post-results order-book rebuild. Since April, hVIVO has announced three meaningful wins: the Traws Pharma influenza challenge trial, the ILiAD BPZE1 pertussis Phase III challenge trial, and a £6m influenza monoclonal antibody contract. The ILiAD contract is the big one. It is hVIVO’s largest human challenge trial to date, involves more than 500 volunteers, starts revenue recognition in H1 2026, and most revenue should be recognized in 2026 and 2027. ILiAD also raised $115m in February to fund BPZE1 Phase III development, which reduces the sponsor-funding overhang that would otherwise dominate this thesis.

The variant perception is that ILiAD is not merely a large one-off contract. It validates pertussis as a challenge-trial category. Conventional pertussis field studies are difficult because outbreaks are unpredictable and baseline vaccination is high. A controlled human challenge model gives vaccine developers faster pivotal efficacy evidence with far fewer participants. If regulators and sponsors accept this pathway, hVIVO’s model library becomes a reusable bottleneck asset, not a project-by-project service business.

The Cidara/Merck precedent matters. hVIVO was involved in the development path around Cidara’s influenza prevention asset, and Merck/MSD completed the Cidara acquisition in January 2026, with a roughly $9bn R&D charge tied to the deal. That does not mean hVIVO owns the drug economics, but it proves the commercial value of fast, controlled influenza efficacy data. Traws and the unnamed monoclonal antibody client are likely trying to generate the same kind of value-inflecting data.

At the live London price of about 9.4p, hVIVO’s market cap is roughly £65m. Back out the £14.3m year-end cash, and the operating platform is valued around £50m before lease liabilities and working-capital movement. That is about 1.0x expected 2026 revenue for a business that has previously shown 26% adjusted EBITDA margins when HCT utilization is high. The stock is not obviously cheap on depressed 2026 earnings, but that is the wrong denominator. The question is whether normalized revenue moves back toward £65m to £80m and whether EBITDA margins recover into the mid-to-high teens as the Canary Wharf facility, labs, FluCamp, and German sites fill.

Retail context is useful because it shows why the mispricing exists. UK message boards are active but skeptical, with investors debating the canceled dividend, cash burn, CFO communication, director selling, Traws funding, and the ever-present AIM “placing” fear. In the US, OPORF is essentially ignored and often stale. The real price discovery is HVO in London, not the OTC line. That creates a messy wrapper, but also explains why a genuine fundamental inflection can sit in plain sight.

The bear case is straightforward: 2025 was not a trough, it was the new normal. HCT demand stays lumpy, the ILiAD trial slips, cancellation fees do not repeat, CRS integration absorbs more cash, and the company needs an equity raise. The 2025 operating cash outflow was material, and deferred income fell sharply, so working capital still deserves respect. A large safety or execution issue in pertussis would also damage the platform narrative.

The base case is that 2026 is a bridge year and 2027 is the earnings inflection. Revenue gets back above £55m in 2026, then £65m-plus as ILiAD, Traws, the £6m influenza contract, CRS cross-selling, and lab work convert. EBITDA margins recover toward 15% to 18%, producing roughly £10m to £12m of EBITDA. A scarce, profitable pharma-services platform with net cash and proprietary challenge models should trade at 10x to 12x EBITDA, not 1x sales.

The bull case is more interesting: pertussis becomes a repeatable regulatory pathway, influenza prophylaxis stays hot after the Merck/Cidara deal, and hVIVO’s expanded platform becomes a strategic asset for a larger CRO or biopharma-services buyer. On £75m to £85m revenue and 20% to 23% EBITDA margins, hVIVO could generate £15m to £19m of EBITDA. A strategic or scarcity multiple of 14x to 16x would justify a share price far above today’s micro-cap range.

The awkward part is pricing OPORF. The OTC quote is stale around $0.086, while the underlying London HVO quote near 9.4p implies roughly $0.125 per ordinary share at GBP/USD around 1.33. I would value it from the London line.

Bear target: 6p, or about $0.08, roughly 36% downside from the HVO-implied current price.
Base target: 18p, or about $0.24, roughly 91% upside.
Bull target: 35p, or about $0.47, roughly 270% upside.

If using the stale OPORF print of $0.086, the base and bull targets imply about 180% and 445% upside, but that partly reflects OTC price staleness rather than pure fundamentals.
